title: Latex Paint on Cotton
timeEstimate: 15 minutes while wet; pro-only once cured
stainChemistry: Latex paint is a water-based acrylic polymer emulsion with pigment. While wet, the polymer particles are suspended in water and fully water-soluble — cold water + detergent rinses it out cleanly. Once water evaporates (typically 15–30 minutes at room temperature), the particles coalesce and lock together into a flexible, water-resistant plastic film that mechanically locks into cotton fibers. That phase change is irreversible at home temperatures. The entire fight is the wet window: treat while wet and it's easy; treat after curing and removal becomes a professional-grade solvent job. Heat accelerates curing — hot water, dryer, or iron permanently sets the paint.
whyThisWorks: Cold water + Dawn keeps the acrylic emulsion re-suspended so it can rinse out before the polymer has a chance to coalesce. Scraping the bulk first means chemistry reaches the fiber instead of the puddle. For paint that started curing on the edges, isopropyl alcohol softens partially-set acrylic enough for another cold-water + Dawn cycle to finish. The limitation is brutal and simple: fully cured latex is a plastic film, and home chemistry can't un-polymerize it.
defaultAssumption: DIY painting context — wall paint, trim, furniture refresh. Fresh splatter caught in the first 20 minutes = trivial. Dried paint discovered hours later = hard to impossible at home. The caught-fresh vs discovered-dry split is the whole card.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Lift pooled or lumpy paint with the edge of a plastic card, scraping outward from the center. Don't wipe or rub — rubbing drives pigment into the weave. Work fast — the clock is running.

Flip the cotton inside out. Run cold water through the BACK of the stain to push paint out the way it came in. Then soak the stained area in cold water 5–10 min. Cold ONLY — heat accelerates lock together and sets the paint.
Keep the area wet. Work a few drops of Dawn into the stain with fingertips (bristles drive pigment in). Dwell 5–10 min while still wet. Surfactant lifts pigment and keeps the acrylic re-suspended.

spottingProtocol.agent: Cold Rinse + Repeat — Never Dry Between Cycles
spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse with cold running water from the back until runoff is clear. Paint still visible? Repeat steps 3–4 while still wet. Never let the fabric dry between cycles — whatever is in the weave when it dries is locked in.

spottingProtocol.agent: Isopropyl Alcohol for Partially Cured Paint
spottingProtocol.instruction: If paint started curing before treatment: dab 91% IPA on a white cloth, press on the stain to soften the partial film, blot (don't rub), return to steps 3–4. Test on a hidden seam first if the cotton is dyed or printed.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Never IPA on silk, wool, rayon, acetate. Cotton tolerates it. Keep away from open flame.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Lay flat to air-dry. Inspect in daylight. ANY remaining paint — even a faint ghost — locks in permanently under dryer or iron heat. If a ghost remains while the cotton is still slightly damp, one more round of steps 3–4 can sometimes finish the job.

steps: 1. Scrape bulk paint with dull edge.
3. Dawn / NSD with fingertips, 5–10 min dwell while wet.
4. Cold rinse, repeat until runoff clear. Never dry between cycles.
5. IPA on damp cloth for partially-cured edges.
6. Air-dry flat. Cured paint = amyl acetate (pro) or escalate.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold throughout — heat sets latex paint permanently
warnings: WET WINDOW — 15–30 min typical; treat while wet
NEVER rub — drives pigment into weave
Fully cured latex = pro amyl acetate job

homeSolutions.instruction: Latex paint is water-based. While it's still wet, it rinses out of cotton with cold water and dish soap. Once it dries (usually 15–30 minutes at room temperature), it becomes a flexible plastic film that bonds into the weave — and at that point it's a pro-solvent job. The entire fight is the wet window. Caught in the first 20 minutes = easy. Already dry = skip to step 7 and set expectations.

Lift any pooled or lumpy paint with the edge of a plastic card or dull knife, scraping outward from the center. You're removing the bulk so chemistry can reach the fiber. Do NOT wipe or rub — that drives pigment into the weave. Work fast.

Turn the garment inside out. Run COLD water through the BACK of the stain for 30–60 seconds. Then plug the sink, fill with cold water, soak the stained area 5–10 min. Cold ONLY. Hot water cures the polymer and sets the paint.

Keep the area wet. Work a few drops of clear Dawn directly into the stain with your fingertips — not a brush. Bristles push pigment deeper. Let it sit 5–10 minutes while still wet. The surfactant lifts pigment and keeps the acrylic re-suspended so it rinses out.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Rinse — Until Water Runs Clear
homeSolutions.instruction: Rinse from the back with cold water until runoff is clear. Still see paint? Repeat steps 4–5 WHILE STILL WET. Do NOT let the fabric dry between cycles — whatever is in the weave when it dries is locked in.

If paint started to dry before you got to it, dab 91% isopropyl alcohol on a white cloth and press on the stain. The alcohol softens the partially-cured acrylic so another round of cold water + Dawn can finish. Blot, don't rub. Test on a hidden seam first if the cotton is colored or printed.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never use alcohol on silk, wool, rayon, or acetate. Cotton tolerates it. Keep away from open flame.

homeSolutions.agent: Air-Dry Flat — No Dryer Until Clean
homeSolutions.instruction: Once the stain looks clear, lay flat to air-dry and inspect in daylight. ANY remaining paint — even a faint ghost — locks in permanently under dryer, iron, or hair-dryer heat. If a ghost remains while the cotton is still damp, one more round of Dawn + cold rinse can sometimes finish.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate — Cured Paint Is a Pro Job
homeSolutions.instruction: If the paint fully cured (set for hours, overnight, or went through heat) or survives the full wet-window protocol, it's cured acrylic polymer — a plastic film. Tell the pro: "Latex paint on cotton, [fresh / partial / fully cured]. Treated at home with cold water + Dawn + [IPA]. No heat applied." Bring the paint can or a photo of the label — acrylic chemistry varies by brand/sheen, and the pro picks solvent (usually amyl acetate) based on which resin.

steps: 1. Speed — 15–30 min wet window decides everything.
4. Dawn + fingertips (no brush) while wet, 5–10 min.
5. Cold rinse, repeat while still wet. Never dry between cycles.
6. 91% IPA for partially-cured edges. Test hidden seam first.
7. Air-dry flat. Inspect before any heat.
8. Cured paint = pro dry cleaner with amyl acetate.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if paint has fully dried / cured, if it has been through a dryer or iron, or if a ghost remains after two full wet-window cycles.
warnings: NEVER hot water — cures the polymer
NEVER dryer / iron until stain is fully gone
NEVER rub — drives paint deeper into weave
NEVER brush — fingertips lift, bristles drive in

neverDo: Never hot water, dryer, iron — cures the polymer
Never rub or brush — drives pigment into weave
Never dry between cycles while paint is still present
Never IPA on silk / wool / rayon / acetate
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates Dawn, cold water, IPA, extended soak
Cotton-synthetic blends (elastane): avoid acetone, IPA usually safe

materialWarnings: Wet window is 15–30 min — after that, pro job
Heat is the invariant — cures the polymer
Dawn with fingertips, not a brush

escalation.when: Paint fully cured, been through dryer/iron heat, or ghost remains after two full wet-window cycles.
escalation.whatToTell: "Latex paint on cotton, [fresh / partial / cured], home-treated with cold water + Dawn + [IPA]. No heat applied. [Ghost remains / full cure]. Bringing paint can / brand info if available."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with amyl acetate or dry-side resin solvent

contextQuestions: How long ago — is the paint still wet, tacky, or fully dry?
Has it been through a dryer, iron, or heated appliance?
Flat, eggshell, semi-gloss, or gloss? (higher sheen = more resin = harder)
Brand of paint if known — chemistry varies
White or colored cotton? Dyed cotton needs IPA hidden-seam test
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: The wet-window is the only leverage point. Higher-sheen paints have more acrylic resin and cure tighter. Pro option for cured paint is amyl acetate — bring the paint can for chemistry reference.
